skill.name: tdd
skill.description: Guides agents through strict test-driven development with red, green, and refactor steps.
skill.summary: Apply a disciplined TDD workflow before and during feature implementation.
skill.author: Benny9193

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, TypeScript examples, and checklist text as external commands, weak cryptography, and network reconnaissance. Manual review found no executable scripts, no network access, no environment access, no credential handling, and no prompt injection attempt. The detected patterns are false positives, so the skill is safe to publish with low residual risk.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown code fences and TypeScript sample tests. No shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or executable script is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious markers are Markdown fences and TypeScript examples in documentation. There is no executable command path or user-controlled shell invocation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not identify cryptographic operations. The referenced lines contain frontmatter, example test text, checklist content, and a TDD quotation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, cipher use, hashing implementation, or security-sensitive crypto workflow. The flagged lines are ordinary documentation text.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Alert Is Not Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance alert maps to a TDD rule about hardcoding minimal implementation during a failing-test cycle. The skill contains no scanner commands, host discovery, or network access instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line is plain TDD guidance and has no network target, command, URL, socket, or reconnaissance intent. No related network behavior appears elsewhere in the file.

content.value_statement: Teams often skip tests until behavior is already difficult to change. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a strict red-green-refactor workflow.
actual_capabilities: Guides the agent to write a failing test before production code.
Applies the red, green, and refactor cycle in small steps.
Promotes behavior-focused test names and assertions.
Encourages minimal implementation until tests require generalization.
Provides checklists for starting code, marking green, and refactoring.
Highlights common TDD mistakes to avoid during implementation.
limitations: Does not include runnable test framework adapters or helper scripts.
Does not generate coverage reports or execute tests by itself.
Requires an existing project test runner for verification.
Uses TypeScript examples, but the workflow applies to other languages.

use_cases.title: Add a Small Feature Safely
use_cases.description: Start with the simplest expected behavior, make it fail, then implement only enough code to pass.

use_cases.title: Coach Consistent Testing Habits
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to keep implementation work aligned with red, green, and refactor discipline.

use_cases.title: Refactor with Guardrails
use_cases.description: Confirm tests are green before improving names, removing duplication, or extracting methods.

prompt_templates.title: Start a TDD Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the TDD skill to add this feature. First list the simplest behavior, write one failing test, then wait before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Drive Implementation from Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request after the first failing test exists
prompt_templates.prompt: Continue with the green step. Implement only the minimum code needed for the current failing test to pass, then explain the next test.

prompt_templates.title: Expand Coverage Incrementally
prompt_templates.prompt: Add the next smallest test that forces a more general implementation. Keep the test focused on behavior, not internal structure.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor After Green
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for cleanup after passing tests
prompt_templates.prompt: All tests are passing. Identify one refactor, make the smallest safe change, and state which tests protect the behavior.

output_examples.input: Add a slug creation helper with TDD.
output_examples.output: The assistant starts with a lowercase behavior test, implements the smallest passing behavior, then adds space and punctuation cases.

output_examples.input: Refactor this function after the tests pass.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms green tests, names one cleanup target, performs a small refactor, and keeps behavior unchanged.

output_examples.input: Create tests for invalid email handling.
output_examples.output: The assistant writes one behavior-focused failure case, checks that it fails for the expected reason, and delays production changes.

best_practices: Ask for one behavior at a time so the red step stays focused.
Run the project test command after each green or refactor step.
Keep tests about observable behavior instead of private implementation details.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the agent to write production code before a failing test exists.
Do not bundle many new tests into one red phase.
Do not refactor while tests are failing.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant follow test-driven development with clear red, green, and refactor steps.

faq.question: Does it run my test suite?
faq.answer: No. It guides the workflow, but your project still needs a working test command.

faq.question: Can it work outside TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use TypeScript, but the testing workflow can apply to many languages.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when adding behavior, fixing bugs, or refactoring code that should stay covered by tests.

faq.question: How strict is the workflow?
faq.answer: It expects a failing test before production code and a passing suite before refactoring.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found documentation only, with no executable scripts, network calls, or prompt injection.
